title: L7: Absolute Cell Referencing
description: Use the $ symbol to lock cell references when copying formulas, and access data stored in other worksheets for BTEC DIT Component 2.

steps.title: Do Now: Cell References
steps.content:
Do now

These questions check the formula and referencing skills you need before learning about absolute cell referencing.
steps.js: createMCQSet('l7-dn',[{q:'A formula in D3 is =B3*C3. It is copied one row down to D4. What does D4 now contain?',options:['=B4*C4','=B3*C3','=B4*C3','=B3*C4'],answer:0,right:'Correct. Relative references update row numbers when copied down.'},{q:'Which formula adds all values in the range B2 to B8?',options:['=SUM(B2:B8)','=COUNT(B2:B8)','=AVERAGE(B2:B8)','=B2+B8 only'],answer:0,right:'Correct. SUM adds every value in the range.'},{q:'Which formula counts how many cells in B2:B10 contain the word Shop?',options:['=COUNTIF(B2:B10,"Shop")','=SUMIF(B2:B10,"Shop")','=COUNT(B2:B10)','=IF(B2="Shop")'],answer:0,right:'Correct. COUNTIF counts cells matching a condition.'},{q:'In a spreadsheet cell reference like C5, what do the letter and number represent?',options:['The column letter and the row number','The row letter and the column number','The sheet name and the cell value','The formula name and the result'],answer:0,right:'Correct. The letter identifies the column and the number identifies the row.'},{q:'What does the formula bar show when you click a cell that contains a formula?',options:['The formula itself (e.g. =A2*B2)','Only the calculated result','The cell name only','Nothing'],answer:0,right:'Correct. The formula bar shows the raw formula, not the formatted display value.'}]);

steps.title: Relative Referencing
steps.content:
Relative cell referencing

The cell references you have used so far are called relative references. When you copy a formula, relative references update automatically to reflect the new row or column position.

CellFormulaWhat happened
C2=A2*B2Original formula
C3=A3*B3Copied down — row numbers increased by 1
C4=A4*B4Copied down again — row numbers increased again

This is useful when every row should use the values from its own row. But there is a problem: what if every row needs to reference the same fixed cell?

Imagine a tax rate is stored in cell B1. You write =B3*B1 in C3. When copied to C4 it becomes =B4*B2 — it no longer references the tax rate in B1. The formula is broken.

CellIntended formulaWhat copying actually produces
C3=B3*B1 ✓Correct — B1 is the tax rate
C4=B4*B1 (intended)=B4*B2 (wrong) — relative copy moved B1 to B2
C5=B5*B1 (intended)=B5*B3 (wrong) — now pointing to a price, not the tax rate

The solution is to use an absolute cell reference so that B1 never changes when copied.
steps.js: createMCQSet('l7-rel',[{q:'A formula =A2+B2 is copied one row down. What is the new formula?',options:['=A3+B3','=A2+B2','=A3+B2','=A2+B3'],answer:0,right:'Correct. Both references are relative, so both row numbers increase by 1.'},{q:'A formula =B3*C3 is copied three rows down. What does the copied cell contain?',options:['=B6*C6','=B3*C3','=B6*C3','=B3*C6'],answer:0,right:'Correct. Each reference shifts by 3 rows.'},{q:'The tax rate is in D1. A formula =B3*D1 is copied from row 3 to row 4. What is the problem?',options:['The copy produces =B4*D2, so D1 (the tax rate) is no longer referenced','Nothing, it works perfectly','D1 is automatically locked','The formula copies as =B3*D1 in both cells'],answer:0,right:'Correct. The relative copy moves D1 to D2, which is no longer the tax rate.'},{q:'When is relative referencing useful?',options:['When each row should calculate using its own values in the same columns','When every row must always reference one fixed cell','When you want to reference a different worksheet','When copying would break the formula'],answer:0,right:'Correct. Relative referencing is ideal when each row needs its own calculation.'}]);

steps.title: Absolute Cell Referencing
steps.content:
Absolute cell referencing

An absolute reference uses a dollar sign $ to lock the column, the row, or both. A locked part never changes when the formula is copied.

ReferenceMeaningWhen copied in any direction
$A$4Column A fixed, row 4 fixedAlways refers to cell A4
$A4Column A fixed, row relativeColumn stays A; row number updates
A$4Column relative, row 4 fixedColumn letter updates; row stays 4
A4Both relativeBoth column and row update (normal)

The most common form for a fixed constant (such as a tax rate, a price per unit, or PI) is $A$4 — locking both the column and the row.

Fixing the tax rate problem with $B$1:

CellFormulaResult
C3=B3*$B$1B3 is relative (price), $B$1 is fixed (tax rate)
C4=B4*$B$1B4 updated to row 4; $B$1 stayed fixed ✓
C5=B5*$B$1B5 updated to row 5; $B$1 stayed fixed ✓

Tip: in Excel, press F4 while your cursor is inside a cell reference to cycle through $A$1, A$1, $A1, and A1 automatically.
steps.js: createMCQSet('l7-abs',[{q:'What does $B$1 mean in a formula?',options:['Both column B and row 1 are fixed','Only column B is fixed','Only row 1 is fixed','Neither is fixed'],answer:0,right:'Correct. A $ before the column letter fixes the column; a $ before the row number fixes the row.'},{q:'A formula must always reference cell D1, no matter where it is copied. Which reference is correct?',options:['$D$1','D1','$D1','D$1'],answer:0,right:'Correct. $D$1 fixes both the column D and row 1.'},{q:'Which reference keeps column B fixed but allows the row number to change when copied down?',options:['$B4','B4','$B$4','B$4'],answer:0,right:'Correct. $B4 locks the column B but the row number (4) is relative and will update.'},{q:'A formula =B3*D$1 is copied one row down. What does the copied formula become?',options:['=B4*D$1','=B4*D$2','=B3*D$1','=B4*D2'],answer:0,right:'Correct. B3 is relative so becomes B4; D$1 has the row fixed at 1 so it stays D$1.'},{q:'How do you quickly add $ signs to a reference while editing a formula in Excel?',options:['Press F4 to cycle through the absolute/relative options','Press F2','Press Ctrl+D','Press Tab'],answer:0,right:'Correct. F4 cycles through $A$1 → A$1 → $A1 → A1.'}]);

steps.title: Practice: Apply Absolute Referencing
steps.content:
Practice: apply absolute referencing

The tax rate (20%) is stored once in cell B1. Write formulas in column C that calculate the tax on each item. Every formula must reference B1 absolutely using $B$1.

CellTask
C4Tax on Pen — multiply the price in B4 by the tax rate in $B$1
C5Tax on Notebook — multiply the price in B5 by the tax rate in $B$1
C6Tax on Folder — multiply the price in B6 by the tax rate in $B$1

Pattern: =B4*$B$1   Each row's price reference changes; the absolute $B$1 never does.
steps.js: (function(){function lcss(u){if(!document.querySelector('link[href="'+u+'"]')){var l=document.createElement('link');l.rel='stylesheet';l.href=u;document.head.appendChild(l);}}function ljs(u,cb){var ex=document.querySelector('script[data-jhncc-src="'+u+'"]');if(ex){if(ex.getAttribute('data-loaded')==='1'){cb();}else{ex.addEventListener('load',function(){cb();});}return;}var s=document.createElement('script');s.setAttribute('data-jhncc-src',u);s.src=u;s.onload=function(){s.setAttribute('data-loaded','1');cb();};s.onerror=function(){alert('The spreadsheet tool could not load. Please refresh the page.');};document.head.appendChild(s);}lcss('assets/css/jsuites.css');lcss('assets/css/jspreadsheet.css');ljs('assets/js/jsuites.js',function(){ljs('assets/js/jspreadsheet.js',function(){ljs('assets/js/jspreadsheet-formula-bar.js?v=21',function(){var data=[['Tax rate (20%)',0.2,''],['','',''],['Item','Price (£)','Tax (£)'],['Pen',2.50,''],['Notebook',5.00,''],['Folder',3.75,'']];var el=document.getElementById('l7-abs-sheet');var sheet=jspreadsheet(el,{data:data,columns:[{width:140},{width:110},{width:120}],minDimensions:[3,6],tableOverflow:true,tableWidth:'100%',toolbar:false,about:false});JHNCCAddFormulaBar(el,sheet);JHNCCAddFormatToolbar(el,sheet);JHNCCAddSheetTabs(el,sheet);document.getElementById('l7-abs-check').onclick=function(){var fb=document.getElementById('l7-abs-fb');var rows=[{row:3,label:'C4',exp:0.50},{row:4,label:'C5',exp:1.00},{row:5,label:'C6',exp:0.75}];var errors=[];rows.forEach(function(c){var raw=String(sheet.getValueFromCoords(2,c.row,false)||'');var val=parseFloat(sheet.getValueFromCoords(2,c.row,true));if(raw.charAt(0)!=='='){errors.push(c.label+' needs a formula starting with =');return;}if(raw.toUpperCase().indexOf('$B$1')===-1){errors.push(c.label+': use $B$1 to reference the tax rate absolutely');}else if(Math.abs(val-c.exp)>0.01){errors.push(c.label+' should equal '+c.exp.toFixed(2)+' (price × 0.2)');}});if(errors.length){fb.className='ex-feedback err';fb.textContent=errors[0];return;}fb.className='ex-feedback ok';fb.textContent='Correct. Each formula uses $B$1 so the tax rate never changes when copied. The price reference (B4, B5, B6) updates automatically for each row.';__markStepComplete();};});});});})();

steps.title: Multiple Worksheets
steps.content:
Multiple worksheets

A spreadsheet workbook is the whole file. Inside the workbook you can have several worksheets (the tabs at the bottom). Each worksheet stores its own data.

TermMeaningExample
WorkbookThe file itselfSalesData.xlsx
WorksheetA tab within the fileSheet1, RawData, Dashboard

You can reference a cell in a different worksheet using the sheet name followed by an exclamation mark, then the cell reference:

Syntax: SheetName!CellRef

FormulaWhat it does
=Constants!B1Reads cell B1 from the sheet called Constants
=RawData!C5Reads cell C5 from the sheet called RawData
=2*$B$1*Constants!A4Multiplies B1 from this sheet by A4 from Constants

A common pattern is to store constants (like PI, VAT rate, or a price list) in a sheet called Constants, then reference them from your calculation sheet. If the constant ever needs updating, you only change it in one place and all formulas update automatically.
steps.js: createMCQSet('l7-sheets',[{q:'What is the difference between a workbook and a worksheet?',options:['A workbook is the whole file; worksheets are the individual tabs inside it','A worksheet is the whole file; workbooks are the individual tabs','They are the same thing','A workbook only contains one sheet'],answer:0,right:'Correct. The workbook is the .xlsx file; worksheets are the tabs you click at the bottom.'},{q:'PI is stored in cell B1 of a sheet called Constants. Which formula reads it?',options:['=Constants!B1','=B1!Constants','=Constants.B1','=B1(Constants)'],answer:0,right:'Correct. The syntax is SheetName followed by ! then the cell reference.'},{q:'Why store a constant like a VAT rate in a separate Constants sheet?',options:['All formulas reference one place, so changing it once updates everything','It makes the file faster to open','It automatically formats all cells as currency','It prevents anyone from viewing the data'],answer:0,right:'Correct. Centralising constants avoids having to update the same value in multiple formulas.'},{q:'Cell A4 needs to be referenced with an absolute column but relative row. How should it be written?',options:['$A4','A$4','$A$4','A4'],answer:0,right:'Correct. $A4 locks the column A while allowing the row number to change when copied.'}]);

steps.title: Full Practice: Circle Measurements
steps.content:
Full practice: circle measurements

The value of PI is stored once in cell B1. Use it with an absolute reference to calculate the circumference of each circle in column C.

Circumference formula: 2 × PI × radius

CellTask
C4Circumference of Circle A — 2 × $B$1 × B4
C5Circumference of Circle B — 2 × $B$1 × B5
C6Circumference of Circle C — 2 × $B$1 × B6

Pattern: =2*$B$1*B4   PI ($B$1) stays fixed; the radius reference (B4, B5, B6) updates for each row.
steps.js: (function(){function lcss(u){if(!document.querySelector('link[href="'+u+'"]')){var l=document.createElement('link');l.rel='stylesheet';l.href=u;document.head.appendChild(l);}}function ljs(u,cb){var ex=document.querySelector('script[data-jhncc-src="'+u+'"]');if(ex){if(ex.getAttribute('data-loaded')==='1'){cb();}else{ex.addEventListener('load',function(){cb();});}return;}var s=document.createElement('script');s.setAttribute('data-jhncc-src',u);s.src=u;s.onload=function(){s.setAttribute('data-loaded','1');cb();};s.onerror=function(){alert('The spreadsheet tool could not load. Please refresh the page.');};document.head.appendChild(s);}lcss('assets/css/jsuites.css');lcss('assets/css/jspreadsheet.css');ljs('assets/js/jsuites.js',function(){ljs('assets/js/jspreadsheet.js',function(){ljs('assets/js/jspreadsheet-formula-bar.js?v=21',function(){var data=[['PI',3.14159,''],['','',''],['Shape','Radius (cm)','Circumference (cm)'],['Circle A',5,''],['Circle B',8,''],['Circle C',10,'']];var el=document.getElementById('l7-full-sheet');var sheet=jspreadsheet(el,{data:data,columns:[{width:110},{width:130},{width:170}],minDimensions:[3,6],tableOverflow:true,tableWidth:'100%',toolbar:false,about:false});JHNCCAddFormulaBar(el,sheet);JHNCCAddFormatToolbar(el,sheet);JHNCCAddSheetTabs(el,sheet);document.getElementById('l7-full-check').onclick=function(){var fb=document.getElementById('l7-full-fb');var rows=[{row:3,label:'C4',rRef:'B4',exp:31.416},{row:4,label:'C5',rRef:'B5',exp:50.265},{row:5,label:'C6',rRef:'B6',exp:62.832}];var errors=[];rows.forEach(function(c){var raw=String(sheet.getValueFromCoords(2,c.row,false)||'');var rawUp=raw.toUpperCase();var val=parseFloat(sheet.getValueFromCoords(2,c.row,true));if(raw.charAt(0)!=='='){errors.push(c.label+' needs a formula starting with =');return;}if(rawUp.indexOf('$B$1')===-1){errors.push(c.label+': reference PI using $B$1 (absolute)');}else if(rawUp.indexOf(c.rRef.toUpperCase())===-1){errors.push(c.label+': include the radius cell '+c.rRef+' in your formula');}else if(Math.abs(val-c.exp)>0.2){errors.push(c.label+' should be approximately '+c.exp.toFixed(2)+' cm');}});if(errors.length){fb.className='ex-feedback err';fb.textContent=errors[0];return;}fb.className='ex-feedback ok';fb.textContent='Correct. PI is locked as $B$1 in every formula. If PI were updated in B1, all three circumferences would recalculate automatically.';__markStepComplete();};});});});})();

steps.title: Check What You Have Learned
steps.content:
Check what you have learned

These questions cover relative and absolute cell referencing and multiple worksheets.
steps.js: createMCQSet('l7-exam',[{q:'What type of reference is A3 in the formula =A3*B3?',options:['Relative — it changes when copied','Absolute — it never changes','Mixed — only the column is fixed','Fixed — the dollar sign locks it'],answer:0,right:'Correct. A3 has no $ so it is a relative reference that updates when copied.'},{q:'A discount rate is in cell C1. Which reference ensures it is always used when the formula is copied down?',options:['$C$1','C1','C$1 only','$C1 only'],answer:0,right:'Correct. $C$1 locks both the column and the row, so the reference never moves.'},{q:'A formula =$D$2*B5 is copied one row down. What does the new formula contain?',options:['=$D$2*B6','=$D$2*B5','=$D$3*B6','=$D$3*B5'],answer:0,right:'Correct. $D$2 is fully absolute so stays the same; B5 is relative so becomes B6.'},{q:'What is the difference between a workbook and a worksheet?',options:['A workbook is the file; worksheets are the tabs inside it','A worksheet is the file; workbooks are the tabs inside it','They mean the same thing','A workbook contains only one worksheet'],answer:0,right:'Correct. The workbook is the .xlsx file; each worksheet is a named tab at the bottom.'},{q:'A sheet called Constants holds the VAT rate in cell B2. Which formula reads it from another sheet?',options:['=Constants!B2','=B2!Constants','=Constants.B2','=VAT(Constants,B2)'],answer:0,right:'Correct. SheetName!CellRef is the syntax for cross-sheet referencing.'},{q:'Which reference allows the row to change but locks the column to B?',options:['$B5','B$5','$B$5','B5'],answer:0,right:'Correct. $B5 locks column B; the row number 5 is relative and will change when copied.'}]);

steps.title: Endless Practice
steps.content:
Endless practice

Answer 5 in a row correctly to finish.
steps.js:
(function(){var bank=[{q:'Which symbol is used to make a cell reference absolute?',o:['$ (dollar sign)','# (hash)','@ (at sign)','& (ampersand)'],a:0},{q:'What does $B$3 mean?',o:['Both column B and row 3 are fixed','Only column B is fixed','Only row 3 is fixed','Neither is fixed'],a:0},{q:'A formula =B4*$C$1 is copied one row down. What is the new formula?',o:['=B5*$C$1','=B4*$C$1','=B5*$C$2','=B4*$C$2'],a:0},{q:'When should you use an absolute reference?',o:['When a cell holds a fixed value such as a rate or constant that all rows share','When every row should use its own values','When you never plan to copy the formula','When you want the formula to show an error'],a:0},{q:'What is a workbook?',o:['The entire spreadsheet file (e.g. SalesData.xlsx)','A single tab within the file','A single cell reference','A type of formula'],a:0},{q:'Which syntax reads cell A1 from a sheet called Data?',o:['=Data!A1','=A1!Data','=Data.A1','=A1(Data)'],a:0},{q:'A tax rate in B1 must be used in every row of column C. What reference should be used?',o:['$B$1','B1','B$1 only','$B1 only'],a:0},{q:'A formula =A$5*B3 is copied one column to the right. What is the new formula?',o:['=B$5*C3','=A$5*C3','=B$5*B3','=A$5*B3'],a:0},{q:'Cell A4 must have its column fixed but row relative. Which is correct?',o:['$A4','A$4','$A$4','A4'],a:0},{q:'Why store a constant in a separate Constants worksheet?',o:['One change updates all formulas that reference it','It makes formulas run faster','It prevents any editing of the value','It automatically formats the cell as currency'],a:0}];var solved=0,c=document.getElementById('l7-endless');function pick(){return bank[Math.floor(Math.random()*bank.length)];}function render(){var q=pick();var opts=q.o.map(function(x,i){return{x:x,i:i};}).sort(function(){return Math.random()-0.5;});c.innerHTML='

Correct in a row: '+solved+' / 5

'+q.q+'

'+opts.map(function(o){return '';}).join('')+'
';c.querySelectorAll('button').forEach(function(btn){btn.onclick=function(){var fb=c.querySelector('.ex-feedback');if(parseInt(btn.dataset.i)===q.a){solved++;fb.className='ex-feedback ok';fb.textContent=solved>=5?'Practice complete.':'Correct. Next question.';if(solved>=5)__markStepComplete();else setTimeout(render,700);}else{solved=0;fb.className='ex-feedback err';fb.textContent='Not quite — streak reset.';setTimeout(render,1000);}};});}render();})();

quizQuestions.q: What type of reference is B4 in the formula =B4*C4?
options: Mixed — only the column is fixed

quizQuestions.q: A VAT rate is stored in B1 and must not change when the formula is copied. Which reference is correct?
The tax rate (20%) is in B1. In C4, write a formula to calculate the tax on the Pen (price in B4) using an absolute reference to $B$1. In C5, do the same for the Notebook.
A formula =$D$1*B3 is copied one row down. What does the new formula become?
What is the difference between a workbook and a worksheet?
options: A workbook is the whole file; worksheets are the individual tabs inside it
A worksheet is the whole file; workbooks are the tabs
A workbook only ever contains one sheet

quizQuestions.q: PI is stored in cell B1 of a sheet called Constants. Which formula reads it from another sheet?
PI (3.14159) is stored in B1. In C4, write a formula for the circumference of Circle A: 2 × $B$1 × B4. In C5, write the same for Circle B.
Which reference keeps the column fixed at B but allows the row to change?
Why is it good practice to store constants (like a tax rate) in a fixed cell and reference it absolutely?
options: Changing the value in one cell updates all formulas that reference it
It prevents the spreadsheet from being saved
It makes the cell invisible to other users
